headings.text: after a photograph of my grandfather, third shift

paragraphs.text: Seventeen poems · industrial grief, inherited silence, the geography of eastern France

“Attention is the rarest and purest form of generosity. To pay attention is to suffer.”
        — Simone Weil

The light here is not light.
            It is the memory of light
            held inside iron a half-second longer
            than the body that poured it.
            My grandfather’s hands —
            I never saw them clean.
            Even washed, they kept the metal
            in the half-moons of the nails,
            a residue more durable than name.

He came home through a door
            that no longer exists,
            into a kitchen whose tiles
            were the color of weak tea,
            and sat. He sat as if sitting
            were a craft taught in apprenticeship.
            My grandmother set bread on the table
            without speaking. The bread also did not speak.

Outside, the foundry continued
            to be a foundry. The hour
            ate the hour before it,
            and the men, walking the road
            in their grey coveralls,
            looked like nothing so much
            as the photograph
            a photograph would later become.

I was not born yet. I have inherited
            the silence between the bread
            and the chair, the way one inherits
            a watch that has stopped —
            knowing the time it shows
            is the time something ended,
            and refusing, still, to wind it.

The digital home of Solenne Vray’s The Hour That Eats Itself.
      Published in a limited first edition, Strasbourg, autumn 2024.

To pay attention is to suffer.” — Simone Weil Plate I — Thionville, anonymous I. Foundry, 1973 after a photograph of my grandfather, third shift The light here is not light. It is the memory of light held inside iron a half-second longer than the body that poured it. My grandfather’s hands — I never saw them clean. Even washed, they kept the metal in the half-moons of the nails, a residue more durable than name. He came home through a door that no longer exists, into a kitchen whose tiles were the color of weak tea, and sat. He sat as if sitting were a craft taught in apprenticeship. My grandmother set bread on the table without speaking. The bread also did not speak. Outside, the foundry continued to be a foundry. The hour ate the hour before it, and the men, walking the road in their grey coveralls, looked like nothing so much as the photograph a photograph would later become. I was not born yet. I have inherited the silence between the bread and the chair, the way one inherits a watch that has stopped — knowing the time it shows is the time something ended, and refusing, still, to wind it.
